title: The changing terrain of racial inequality in Trinidad and Tobago
abstract: Trinidad and Tobago has maintained a strong economic position within the Americas. However, the country still has relatively high poverty rates, and its economic, political, and social structures are based on racism despite boasting the largest ethnoracially diverse population in the Anglophone Caribbean. How have ethnoracial differences in socioeconomic outcomes evolved post-independence in this twin-island nation? To address this question, I utilized 1970-2011 census data to investigate trends in socioeconomic outcomes by self-identified race. The results revealed that: (1) the significant and persistent White/Black disparities have gradually reduced over time, (2) more progress has been made in closing the gap between Blacks and East Indians, although parity has not been achieved, however, (3) Trinidad proves an exception regarding the structural position of East Indians and assumption of singular Black disadvantage. These findings are compared to racebased social stratifications found in the United States, Latin America, and other Caribbean countries. Thus, this study expands the literature on the contextuality of race and further challenges our understanding of racial inequity beyond the Black/White binary of the United States and the Black/mulatto/White ternary system of Latin America and the Caribbean.
journal: Research in Social Stratification and Mobility
